figures.caption: Figure 5: Sensitivity to retained rank r in SGLT (hard gate with τ chosen to keep top-r components). Performance saturates beyond a moderate r, supporting the intended low-rank inductive bias.

Table 5: Unimodal and X-Fi multi-config baselines on MM-Fi (mean±std, 3 seeds). Baselines run without DP for the strongest comparison; UMEDA runs at ϵ = 2.0 SP-DP. UMEDA outperforms all baselines despite the privacy disadvantage, demonstrating that operator-space alignment compensates for DP noise.

Figure 9: Effect of clipping bound C (Eq. (7)) on utility and stability. Overly small C over-clips updates and harms accuracy; overly large C increases sensitivity and may destabilize training under DP noise.

Table 1: Main results on MM-Fi with N = 100 clients (mean±std over 3 seeds). Metrics: 3D pose estimation (MPJPE, mm)↓, action recognition (Top-1 Acc, %)↑, localization (RMSE, cm)↓. Privacy baselines and UMEDA use the same privacy budget (e.g., ϵ = 2.0, δ = 10−5). Centralized is an oracle upper bound and excluded from best/second-best marking.

Figure 2: Architecture and mechanisms of UMEDA. Left: end-to-end pipeline. Heterogeneous clients (Wi-Fi time series, LiDAR point clouds, etc.) apply SGLT to produce kernel updates ∆Mk, privatize them via SP-DP, and upload to the server, which aggregates with Diff-GNO on Θ0 = vec(∆M̃) and FedAvg on the rest, then broadcasts θ. Right: (A) SGLT retains low-rank shared semantics (blue bars) and suppresses high-frequency sensor noise (purple bars) on the attention kernel. (B) Diff-GNO denoises diverse client operator updates (t=T , noisy) into a globally aligned operator (t=0) via reverse-time diffusion. (C) SP-DP adds more noise in the null (sensitive) subspace and less in the signal (useful) subspace, preserving utility under (ϵ, δ)-DP.

Figure 10: Robustness under graph-size distribution shifts induced by discretization heterogeneity. We perturb the client-type mix and token/graph resolutions; UMEDA remains stable due to operator-level fusion in SGLT/Diff-GNO.

Figure 8: Gradient-inversion reconstruction attack against client updates under three regimes: raw gradients (no privacy), isotropic DP at ϵ = 2.0, and SP-DP at the matched ϵ = 2.0. For each regime we show, per modality (RGB, LiDAR, mmWave, Wi-Fi CSI): (top) the ground-truth multi-modal sample with its 3D pose; (middle) the attacker’s reconstruction from the uploaded gradient; (bottom) per-pixel SSIM heatmap against the ground truth. Without privacy, the attacker recovers scene layout, body silhouette, and pose across all four modalities (mean SSIM 0.82). Isotropic DP partially blurs the scene but leaves recognizable skeletons and LiDAR/mmWave point structure (SSIM 0.41). SP-DP reduces all modalities to structureless noise (SSIM 0.12): scene, subject identity, pose, and action are all unrecoverable, while downstream utility is preserved (Table 1).

Figure 7: Fine-grained privacy–utility frontier on MM-Fi and RELI11D under (ϵ, δ)-DP with δ = 10−5. UMEDA (SP-DP) maintains a usable signal subspace down to ϵ = 0.25, whereas isotropic DP-FedAvg collapses below ϵ = 1.
Table 11: Client scalability on MM-Fi. Per-round server compute is dominated by score-model training on Rr2=256 updates and is invariant to K. Final accuracy improves modestly with K.

Figure 11: Distribution alignment over communication rounds. We report a cross-client discrepancy proxy (Maximum Mean Discrepancy, MMD) computed on the shared latent space; Diff-GNO reduces drift compared to optimizer-only baselines.

Figure 6: Gate design ablation. We compare hard gating to soft gating with sigmoid temperature β (Eq. (4)). Smaller β approaches hard thresholding; moderate β stabilizes training while preserving spectral selectivity.
